content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 19, 2016

Colleen Winchester
Assistant City Attorney
City of Cupertino
20410 Town Center Lane, Suite 210
Cupertino, CA 95014-3255

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 16-030

Dear Ms. Winchester:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090.¹ Please note that we do not advise on any
other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

For advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Clara County District
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written
response from either entity. We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090,
the advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding brought against any individual other than the
requestor." (Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Under the Act and Section 1090, may Councilmember Sinks participate in the City of
Cupertino's decision to lease a city-owned building to the local chapter of Rotary Club, in light of
the fact that the Councilmember serves as a Director of the organization?

CONCLUSION

Barring any other interests in the decision, Councilmember Sinks does not have a
disqualifying interest in Rotary as an uncompensated officer of the nonprofit, tax-exempt,
organization under either the Act or Section 1090. Accordingly, the City of Cupertino may enter
into the lease agreement with the organization and Councilmember Sinks is not precluded from

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-030
Page No. 2

taking part in the decision so long as the fact that he is currently serving as a Director for the
organization is noted in the official record.

FACTS

Rod Sinks is a Councilmember for the City of Cupertino, where he also serves as an
uncompensated Director on the Board of Rotary Club of Cupertino, Inc. ("Rotary"). Rotary is a
registered 501(c)(4) nonprofit organization, the local branch of a worldwide network of service
organizations which provide "humanitarian service […] and help build goodwill and peace in the
world." More specifically, Rotary's work includes supporting the residents and City of Cupertino
through volunteer service, like organizing blood drives, donating furniture to the City's Senior
Center, and providing grants to local teachers. According to Rotary's bylaws, its Directors are
"elected to manage the financial affairs and set the operational policies of the club."

In current negotiations with the City, Rotary seeks a non-exclusive lease on a City-owned
building in exchange for renovating the building through its volunteer efforts. The issue is whether
the City may enter into a lease agreement with Rotary under the Act and Section 1090, even though
Councilmember Sinks sits on Rotary's Board.

ANALYSIS

Political Reform Act

As a public official, Councilmember Sinks is subject to the Act's conflict of interest
provisions. Under Section 87100, a public official may not make, participate in making, or use his
official position to influence a governmental decision in which he has a financial interest. A public
official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the meaning of the Act if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Section 87103 identifies interests
from which a conflict of interest may arise, including:

•  An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

•  An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

•  An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

•  An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $460 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

•  An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family, also known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

File No. A-16-030
Page No. 3

A nonprofit organization such as Rotary is not considered a "business entity" for purposes
of the Act. (Section 82005.) Thus, as an uncompensated Director of Rotary, it does not appear that
Councilmember Sinks has an interest in the organization as a source of income. Likewise, because
he is unpaid, the proposed lease does not implicate the Councilmember's personal finances.
Similarly, Councilmember Sinks does not have an interest in the real property at issue, which the
City owns and would lease to Rotary at no expense.

Assuming he has no other interests implicated by the decision, Councilmember Sinks does
not appear to have a potentially disqualifying interest in a decision regarding the lease under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act.

Section 1090

In addition to the Act, we must also determine whether Councilmember Sinks has a
potentially disqualifying interest in a decision regarding the lease under Section 1090. Section 1090
generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making contracts in
which they are financially interested. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract
that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and
equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649).

In this case, there is no question that Councilmember Sinks is a public officer subject to the
provisions of Section 1090, that the City's lease with Rotary would constitute a public contract for
purposes of Section 1090, or that Councilmember Sinks is presumed to be involved in the making
of all contracts by the City. The determinative question is whether Councilmember Sinks has a
financial interest in the contract under Section 1090, since Rotary is a nonprofit and the
Councilmember is unpaid volunteer.

Sections 1091 and 1091.5 set forth various statutory categories of "remote interests" and
"noninterests" under Section 1090. If an official has only a "remote interest," the contract may be
made if (1) the official in question discloses his or her financial interest in the contract, (2) the
interest is noted in the public entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any
participation in the making of the contract. (Section 1091(a), 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108
(2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) If a "noninterest" is present, the official in question
may participate in the contract and disclosure is not generally necessary. (City of Vernon v. Central
Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160
(2001).)

In pertinent part, Section 1091.5(a) includes under "noninterests":

"(8) That of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit, tax-exempt
corporation, which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the
body or board or to which the body or board has a legal obligation to give particular
consideration, and provided further that this interest is noted in its official records.

For purposes of this paragraph, an officer is "noncompensated" even though
he or she receives reimbursement from the nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation for

File No. A-16-030
Page No. 4

necessary travel and other actual expenses incurred in performing the duties of his
or her office."

You have indicated that Rotary is a tax-exempt organization and Councilmember Sinks is
uncompensated. As such, the question is whether one of Rotary's primary purposes supports the
functions of the City. You also observe that "one of Rotary's primary functions is to support the
City," through volunteer service, such as sponsoring a Fall Festival and hosting the State of the City
address. In a recent advice letter, we found that Section 1090 did not prohibit a city from leasing a
city-owned building to a local post of the Veterans of Foreign Wars (VFW), even though the city's
mayor was Vice-Commander of the organization, since the VFW was a nonprofit organization that
provided veteran services to the city's residents (Sullivan Advice Letter, A-15-121). Similarly, we
find that Rotary's volunteer services support an important function of the City of Cupertino. Thus,
Councilmember Sinks has a "non-interest" in Rotary, and is not prohibited from taking part in the
City's decisions regarding the lease so long as his role as a Board member is noted in the City's
official records.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Toren A. Lewis
Counsel, Legal Division

TAL:jgl
